title: Variable Scope 🔭
description: Understand where variables live and die.

slides.title: The Memory Bubble 🫧
slides.content:
Variables created inside a function are completely **invisible** to the rest of the program.

This is called **Local Scope**. The `{}` braces act as thick concrete walls. When the function finishes, everything inside those walls is deleted from memory!

slides.title: Scope Error
slides.content: Try to guess what happens when `main` tries to print the `secretCode` variable that was created inside the `hack()` function.

quizData.question: Why does the C compiler throw a 'undeclared identifier' error if `main()` tries to access a variable made inside another function?

options.text: Because variables have 'Local Scope' and cannot be seen outside the {} braces where they were created.
Because the variable was a secret.

quizData.explanation: Variables are strictly born and then destroyed within the `{}` braces they are defined in. They cannot be seen from the outside.

slides.title: Global Variables 🌍
slides.content:
You *can* create a variable outside of ALL functions. This is called a **Global Variable**.

Every function in the entire file can see and modify a Global Variable. But beware! Overusing globals is considered bad practice because any function can accidentally break the data.

slides.title: Global Example
slides.content: Notice how `playerScore` is declared at the top, outside of any `{}`.
